content.full_text:
June 22, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: REQUEST FOR INFORMAL ASSISTANCE; INTERPRETATION OF REGULATION 18526.1; REIMBURSEMENT OF EXPENDITURES MADE BY CANDIDATE FROM PERSONAL FUNDS FOR OFFICEHOLDER EXPENSES

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I write to request informal assistance with respect to the interpretation of Regulation 18526.1. That regulation deals with expenditures for non-"campaign expenses" made by a candidate from his or her personal funds. These expenditures may then be reimbursed by the candidate's campaign bank account if certain procedures are adhered to by the candidate. In essence, these are "officeholder" expenses rather than expenses for seeking election to office. (See Regulation 18526.1(a)(1) and (b)(1), both of which make cross-reference to Regulation 18525(a).)

Regulation 18526.1(b)(3) and (d) prohibit a candidate from being reimbursed for "officeholder" expenses paid from personal funds unless reimbursement is made within 45 days after the "expenditures are paid." Recently, one of my clients was advised that this means that if a personal credit card is used, the reimbursement must occur within 45 days of the use of the card rather than 45 days of the payment from personal funds for the credit card bill.

I am writing to request that you review the interpretation of this provision of the regulation. Often credit card charges do not appear on bills for some time into the future. It is simply impractical to seek reimbursement within 45 days of the date of use of the card. Furthermore, the candidate has not yet actually expended his or her personal funds until the credit card bill is paid. Since the term used in Regulation 18526.1(b)(3) is "paid" and not "made" or "incurred," I would urge that the 45-day time period begin to run when the expenditure is actually paid by the candidate out of his or her personal funds.

Scott Hallabrin  
June 22, 1990  
Page 2

There is no strong policy reason why this interpretation should not be permitted. These expenditures are not for the purpose of seeking election; hence, they would not give a candidate an advantage over his or her opponent(s). Secondly, unlike campaign expenses, which are required by the statute (Section 85201(d) and (e)) to be made through the campaign bank account, these are officeholder expenses which the candidate may make directly.

Furthermore, the candidate could lawfully delay reimbursement if he or she wished to do so by simply calling the initial expenditure a loan to his or her own committee which would be repaid later. Since a candidate may make unlimited loans or contributions to his or her own committee, there would be nothing to prohibit this. But why go through the paperwork?

It seems the much simpler approach, and one which appears intended by the language of the regulation, is to allow reimbursement of "officeholder" expenses within 45 days of the date the expense is in fact paid from the candidate's own funds.

I and my client will appreciate your timely response to this request.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[Signature]
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

/ccz
